judge_scores.justification: Tests two related but distinct concepts (homeostasis maintenance and adaptation to stress changes).
Tests multiple facts: definition, mechanism (gene activation), and outcomes (protein synthesis, organelle production).
Tests two facts: the mechanism of hyperplasia and the source of new cells.
Perfect card testing a single fact about which tissues undergo only hypertrophy.
Slightly lacks context about what 'permanent tissues' means without prior knowledge.
Perfect card testing a single exception to hyperplasia-cancer progression.
Tests two related facts: progression to dysplasia and eventual cancer development.
Tests three distinct facts: definition of atrophy, its cause, and two mechanisms of size reduction.
Perfect card testing single mechanism of cell number decrease in atrophy.
Tests two mechanisms (ubiquitin-proteosome degradation and autophagy) for cell size decrease.
Tests both the definition of metaplasia and its most common location.
Tests three facts: specific cell type changes, location, and causative factor.
Perfect card testing single fact about metaplasia reversibility.
Tests two related progressions: to dysplasia and to cancer.
Tests both the definition and the typical cellular characteristic of dysplasia.
Tests two potential causes of dysplasia rather than one.
Perfect card testing single fact about dysplasia irreversibility.
Tests two definitions rather than focusing on one distinction.
Tests both what CIN represents and its relationship to cancer.
Perfect card testing single fact about dysplasia reversibility.
Perfect card with clear, atomic definition of aplasia.
Tests both the mechanism and the outcome of hypoplasia.
Perfect card testing single concept about when cellular injury occurs.
Perfect card testing single fact about neuronal susceptibility to ischemia.
Perfect card testing single fact about skeletal muscle resistance to ischemia.
Tests two related concepts (hypoxia's effect and the mechanism) but they're closely linked enough to be acceptable.
Perfect card - tests single definition with no issues.
Tests both the definition and the specific cutoff value, reducing atomicity.
Tests multiple facts: normal PaO2, decreased O2 saturation, and CO binding affinity.
Clinical sign is relevant but less critical than pathophysiology concepts.
Tests both the oxidation state change and its functional consequence.
Tests two clinical signs together, though they're closely related manifestations.
Oversimplified - reversible injury includes more than just swelling (e.g., fatty change).
Oversimplified - irreversible injury has multiple features beyond membrane damage.
Perfect card - clear cause-and-effect relationship.
Perfect card - clear mechanism and consequence.
Perfect card - single clear consequence of membrane damage.
Perfect card - specific molecule and its role.
Tests three separate nuclear changes and their definitions in one card.
Lists three items but as a single conceptual unit of nuclear changes in cell death.
Tests three characteristics of necrosis: cell groups, acute nature, and inflammation.
Tests multiple facts: ATP-dependence, single cells, and absence of inflammation.
Compares two concepts but focuses on one key distinction.
Tests four separate characteristics of coagulative necrosis in one card.
Perfect card - single association between condition and necrosis type.
Tests both appearance and mechanism, though closely related.
Lists three organs but as examples of one concept.
Tests two descriptive features but they describe the same appearance.
Perfect card - single pathognomonic association.
Tests appearance, calcium deposition, and saponification mechanism - three distinct facts.
Perfect card testing a single, clear medical fact about fat necrosis locations.
Excellent card with precise terminology and single concept focus.
Tests two related but distinct facts (location and appearance) in one card.
Clear and atomic card about specific conditions showing fibrinoid necrosis.
Tests three facts: caspase function and two separate enzyme activities.
Perfectly atomic card testing single consequence of Bcl2 inactivation.
Tests two distinct triggers rather than a single fact.
Tests two characteristics (shrinkage and intact membrane) rather than one.
Perfectly atomic card about protease function.
Perfectly atomic card about endonuclease function.
Tests the relationship between two events rather than a single fact.
Tests both ligand-receptor binding and downstream effect.
Tests two separate mechanisms (perforins and granzymes) in one card.
Tests three distinct facts: definition, and two types of damage mechanisms.
Tests enzyme name, substrate, product, and location in one card.
Excellent atomic card about a single mechanism of reperfusion injury.
Tests three facts: protein structure, configuration type, and staining property.
Perfect atomic card about specific histological appearance.
Clear and atomic card linking AL amyloid to plasma cell disorders.
Perfectly atomic card about AA amyloid etiology.
Tests three separate clinical manifestations in one card.
